Meet John and Erma Evans, residents of a small rural town in Maine who have embraced clean energy solutions in their home. Over 20 years they developed a vision for building their dream home powered by the sun and innovative clean energy technologies. The Evanses have embraced electric cars because they are practical, cheaper to fuel and maintain, and help reduce pollution. They live at the top of a very steep dirt road and have found that their electric car and truck can get the job done, lugging, tugging, or just getting around town. A rooftop solar system powers their electric vehicles, which are then able to provide them with backup power if it's ever needed while still being able to travel everywhere they need to get, including across the country. ✨ In Costa Rica’s Playa Ostional, one of nature’s greatest spectacles unfolds: the arribada. For a few magical days, tens of thousands of Olive Ridley sea turtles emerge from the ocean together to nest. 🐢 🌍 This rare phenomenon only happens in a handful of places on Earth — Costa Rica, Mexico & India — and Ostional is one of the largest, most important nesting sites. Each turtle lays nearly 100 eggs, continuing a cycle of life that’s been happening for generations. 💚 Protecting this magic matters. Rising pollution, plastics, and climate change threaten these turtles — but change begins right where we are. Start small: pick up trash, recycle, reduce single-use plastics. Every act of care ripples outward. 🌊 And for us, it’s even more special — because this is home. The place where we built Mindful Waves, and where we’re reminded daily that people and nature can still live in harmony. ✨ 📸 Gracias a nuestro amigo @heiner_castillo___m fotografo local por compartir los videos!

Nuclear is the power source of the future ... and it has been for about 50 years. Could this time be different? In 1967, America expected to operate 1,000 nuclear power plants by the year 2000. But today, the actual number is just 54. What happened? For decades, nuclear energy was subject to (usually incorrect) fears about its safety. Lately, however, those fears are fading. Support for nuclear is nearing an all-time high. And there are understandable reasons for that. A single uranium pellet generates as much energy as 120 gallons of oil or a ton of coal. And we’re going to need a lot of power. The U.S. Department of Energy estimates that the world will use as much as 75% more electricity by the year 2050, partially because of the demands of energy-intensive technologies like AI. That’s why many tech companies are now leading the charge to develop new nuclear resources. It’s also the case that nuclear is the most viable technology to both provide abundant energy and reduce carbon emissions. In fact, in places like Japan and New York the closure of nuclear plants — despite being touted as an environmental victory — has actually led to *increased* carbon emissions. And because nucle …

👀🦭 yes I know this is a female but it was too funny not to post with this caption..🤣 Sea lions are intelligent, social marine mammals found along coastlines and islands around the world. Known for their playful behavior and loud vocalizations, sea lions are often spotted basking in the sun or diving gracefully through the waves. Unlike true seals, sea lions have external ear flaps and use their strong front flippers to “walk” on land, giving them much more mobility. They’re also excellent swimmers, capable of diving hundreds of feet deep and holding their breath for up to 10 minutes. In the wild, sea lions play a vital role in maintaining the balance of marine ecosystems. However, they face growing threats from climate change, pollution, overfishing, and entanglement in plastic waste. By learning more about sea lions and how they live, we can better protect the oceans they depend on—and so do we. #SeaLions #MarineLife #OceanWildlife #AnimalFacts #WildlifeEducation #ConservationMatters #ProtectOurOceans #EcoAwareness

A new EPA plan would let polluters spew as much greenhouse gas as they want, but it goes against both science and the law. Back in 2009, the EPA concluded that greenhouse gases, and the climate change they power, threaten public health. It was the overwhelming scientific consensus at the time—and it still is. But climate deniers in the White House and the EPA are trying to overturn that decision, called the “endangerment finding.” This has served as the legal foundation for safeguards that cut greenhouse gas pollution from oil and gas drilling, cars and trucks, power plants, and more. Repealing the endangerment finding flies in the face of science and would make it harder to combat the climate crisis. EPA, we won’t stop fighting for the planet. If we need to, we’ll see you in court.

Selling public lands is harmful to wildlife and ecosystems because these lands provide critical habitats, clean water sources, and intact ecosystems that support diverse animal and plant life. When public lands are sold to private owners or developers, protections are lost—private landowners are not required to manage land for wildlife or environmental health, and are less restricted in how they use the land. This often leads to habitat destruction, fragmentation, and pollution from development, resource extraction, or luxury housing projects, which can displace wildlife and permanently damage fragile ecosystems. Once these lands are sold and altered, the natural habitats and ecological functions they provide are often lost forever! #publiclands #notforsale #notoneacre #donotsale #handsoff #sportsmen #hiking #camping #conservation #wildplaces #publiclands

Despite growing access to medical information, a new Cleveland Clinic survey reveals many Americans still don’t fully understand what truly puts their hearts at risk. In the national survey conducted in early 2025, researchers found that: Over 40% of respondents did not recognize alcohol as a heart health risk More than 90% were unaware that many popular supplements and vitamins may offer no cardiovascular benefit—or worse, pose harm Nearly half didn’t know sleep deprivation significantly increases heart disease risk And 62% had no idea air pollution can damage the heart 💬 “This disconnect between public understanding and clinical evidence is concerning,” said Dr. Samir Kapadia, Chair of Cardiovascular Medicine at Cleveland Clinic. “We need to shift how we talk about prevention—not just exercise and diet, but also hidden factors like sleep, pollution, and substance use.” Another alarming finding: 22% of Americans admit they occasionally skip their prescribed heart medications. These lapses, even when infrequent, can seriously increase risk for stroke, heart attack, or worsening heart failure. ❤️ What this means: Education is prevention. Awareness saves lives. In just 70 years, over 130,000 breeding pairs of African penguins have disappeared, leaving the species on the brink of extinction. 🇿🇦 Just 3% of the original population remains, and every year, these dwindling numbers are reduced even further, thanks to overfishing and rampant pollution. Off the coast of South Africa, in Algoa Bay, around 700 breeding pairs are struggling to survive in a vital colony. But as potential parents die from starvation, their eggs are left abandoned and at risk. Our partner, the Southern African Foundation for the Conservation of Coastal Birds (SANCCOB), runs a crucial program to save these eggs, incubating and hand-rearing chicks until they’re ready to be released into protected areas. As countless eggs are in danger, they need all the help they can get. Every rescued egg is essential to the survival of the entire species, and we must help them save as many as possible. 🔗 Take action today to save the African penguins: https://bit.ly/ASISPR03F. These big polluters want Trump to give them a two-year pass on pollution rules Organizations representing heavily polluting industries are taking advantage of the Trump Administration’s offer to exempt them from hazardous air pollution control rules for two years. The American Fuel and Petrochemical Manufacturers and the American Chemistry Council recently sent a letter to the Environmental Protection Agency asking for a two-year exemption to air toxics rules the Biden Administration tightened last year to protect public health. The rules apply to 218 facilities that make up some of the largest petrochemical complexes in the U.S. Exempting companies from these rules could expose the public to more than 12 million pounds per year of hazardous air pollutants – chemicals known to cause cancer, birth defects, or other serious health problems. ‍ The request for the exemption came after the Trump Administration on March 12 offered to give heavily-polluting facilities a break from regulations under the Clean Air Act.

Share these fun facts to conserve these magnificent creatures: 🐋 Humpback whales live in all oceans around the world and have one of the largest migrations of any mammal on the planet 🐋 Scientists have discovered that like human languages, humpback communication systems are culturally transmitted, learned from others in the community and passed down over the generations. 🐋 Male humpback whales sing long, elaborate songs, which are composed of a variety of sounds strung together in repeated phrases and themes 🐋 Whales are ecosystem engineers, cycling nutrients from one place to another 🐋 In life, each great whale captures carbon from the atmosphere, sequestering an estimated 33 tons of C02 on average 🐋 After death, their carcasses bloom into miniature ecosystems, supporting more than 200 species of aquatic life 🐋 A minimum of 300,000 whales and dolphins are killed each year due to fisheries bycatch, while others succumb to threats including loss of habitat, shipping collisions, vessel-based harassment, fuel pollution, and underwater noise. In his report, Dr. Cholakian provides a detailed examination of the Siberian magma plume — a rising flow of hot magma beneath the Siberian Platform. He emphasizes the need to analyze the relationship between the Siberian plume's activity and the condition of the world's oceans, including the emergence of new dead zones, increased acidification, and the disruption of the ocean's role as a planetary climate regulator. The presentation puts forward a concrete solution to prevent a potential global catastrophe: the process of controlled degassing of the Siberian plume, which requires the close collaboration of the international scientific community. The material is based on a comprehensive analysis of climate data, long-term observations conducted using open sources, and the use of advanced methods of mathematical modeling. #climate #science #geodynamics #ecology

Myth or Fact? When you avoid burning wood, you help your community breathe easier. (fact!) During Bay Area winters, wood smoke from fireplaces and stoves is the leading source of particulate matter pollution, significantly impacting air quality and health. Particulate matter, or "soot," consists of tiny solid or liquid particles. “Fine” particles under 2.5 micrometers are especially harmful as they bypass the nose and throat, reaching deep into the lungs and even the bloodstream where they can cause more severe health issues.

Researchers are discovering that a healthier environment means healthier aging. Particulate matter from wildfire smoke and other pollutants can penetrate deep into our lungs and bloodstream. These tiny particles—known as PM2.5—affect the air we breathe and pose a significant risk to cognitive health. To learn more about environmental pollutants and brain health, tap the link in our bio. 🔗

As you look to stay warm this winter, keep in mind the impact wood burning has on our air quality. From November to February, smoke from wood burning is the largest source of air pollution in the Bay Area, accounting for about one-third of fine particle pollution, or PM2.5. Electric lawn equipment is a climate solution. Electric lawn mowers have zero emissions during their use. In contrast, gas-powered lawn mowers make up about 5% of air pollution in the US annually. A 2015 study by the EPA found that in 2011, gasoline-powered lawn and garden equipment emitted approximately 26.7 million tons of air pollutants and accounted for 24%−45% of all nonroad gasoline emissions in the US. Another study estimated that cutting grass for one hour is equivalent to driving a car 100 miles. #lawnmower #fall #lawnmowers

Methane leaks are like an invisible climate change bomb blowing up around us all the time: Methane is 86x more potent at warming our planet than carbon dioxide and is responsible for at least 30% of global temperature rises. But most methane emissions are just preventable leaks caused by carelessness and poor maintenance by oil and gas companies, from storage tanks, pipelines, and pumping stations. Your governor can take action now to reduce or eliminate these preventable methane emissions in your state, by implementing basic EPA rules. Add your voice and tell your governor to take action on your state’s methane policy today.

As a Richmond City Council candidate, I don't take contributions from Chevron or any other corporation. I will do all I can to make them lower the amount of PM2.5 and other pollutants they pump into our air -- so more of our kids can breathe easy again. On Clean Air Day I attended the city-sponsored public forum on asthma. One terrible statistic I learned is that kids in Richmond are twice as likely to get asthma as kids in California overall. Nearly 30% of Richmond youth are asthmatic. Asthma has multiple causes, but one important cause is the amount of tiny pollution particles in the air — the most important ones are called 2.5 micrometer particulate matter or PM2.5. And in Richmond, nearly two-thirds of the PM2.5 in the air comes from the Chevron refinery (Source: BAAQMD).

Toribio Aguinda is one of the most courageous people I know. He’s a leader of the Cofan Indigenous group, on my left here, and he was just a child when Texaco arrived in Ecuador in the 1960s. 🌿 3 Ways You Can Help Protect Mangroves 🌊 Mangroves are essential to the health of the Gulf of Mexico, and you can make a difference in preserving these vital ecosystems. Here are the three actions you can take: Secure your trash. Because mangroves sit at the edge of the sea, they often become a trap for marine debris. Make sure your trash on and off the water is secure and bring an extra container to collect trash when you see it. Reduce Runoff and Pollution. Minimize the use of fertilizers and chemicals in your yard. Runoff from these substances can harm mangrove habitats and the marine life that depends on them. Opt for natural alternatives and properly dispose of hazardous waste. Support Local Conservation Efforts. Get involved with or donate to organizations working to protect and restore mangrove forests. Your support helps fund crucial research and conservation projects that safeguard these ecosystems. Every action counts when it comes to protecting our mangroves and the vital role they play in the Gulf of Mexico. 🐟🦖 Dinos in the Delaware! The Atlantic Sturgeon is a living fossil from the age of dinosaurs -- as big as a Great White shark and covered in spiked bony plates like a Stegosaurus. For most of its life, it swims the seas, feeding on worms, shrimp, crabs, and other invertebrates on the ocean floor. They spawn in freshwater channels like the Delaware River, which also serves as a nursey where young sturgeons may take years to mature. These massive fish once supported a booming caviar industry in the Delaware Bay, but overfishing collapsed populations in the early 1900s. They were poised to make a comeback in the 1990’s but sadly new threats emerged: ship strikes, trawl fishing, commercial dredging, mass habitat destruction. Wildfire smoke can affect air quality in areas thousands of miles away from active wildfires. Monitoring air quality indexes and advisories are crucial to minimizing exposure to harmful pollutants. Just as you check the weather forecast each day, check the air quality index for your area. The U.S. Air Quality Index (AQI) measures air pollution according to the Clean Air Act of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). The AQI has six color-coded categories to help you quickly determine the air quality in your community. • Green — Air pollution poses little to no risk, and the air quality is satisfactory. • Yellow — Air quality is acceptable, but there may be a risk for people sensitive to air pollution. • Orange — While most people are unlikely to be affected, people sensitive to air pollution may experience health effects. • Purple — The risk of health effects is increased for everyone. • Maroon — Everyone is likely to be affected by the hazardous conditions.

The findings are clear: RNG-powered trucks stand out as the best available solution. While electric trucks have zero tailpipe emissions, high costs and infrastructure challenges limit their adoption. Renewable diesel provides an improvement over petroleum diesel but doesn’t reduce emissions as much as other options. Yesterday, the Nebraska Legislature's revenue committee held a public hearing to discuss LB 503. LB 503 would allow counties in Nebraska to be designated as American Energy Friendly. This designation would give the ability to tax privately-owned, renewable energy generation facilities. Nebraska counties could seek designation as American Energy Friendly either through a resolution of the county board or by a vote of the people. What they are talking about is giving Nebraska counties the ability to tax the owners of solar farms and wind farms to help generate new revenue for those counties. It is not clear whether the counties would maintain zoning and approval jurisdiction once the county is declared American Energy Friendly. Some experts believe LB 503 would allow the developers, many which are foreign owned, to bypass county zoning approval for these projects. It should also be noted that the counties would not be required to put this up to a vote of the people. As I see it, LB 503 would give county boards across the state even more incentive to approve new wind and solar energy farms. I grew up doing farm work, helping to raise sheep for the fair, baling hay, helping milk cows, and planting tobacco. So, I am no stranger to hard work. My wife and I decided to buy our farm when we had our first child because we believed it was important to raise kids on a farm. We are a small farm with only 56 acres, and we received a flier in the mail about a solar company that was developing adjacent to our property. We figured that this would increase our tillable acreage by more than half. So we decided, yes, let's do it. Now, we pasture our sheep at the solar facility and can still harvest our hay in the winter. Essentially, we have a larger farm. It is a win-win for us, and the developer is thrilled about our progress on the site by the grazing of the sheep." -Beau Stafford, Wisconsin Farmer #FarmToPower #Support …

The State of Arizona Will Compensate Local Residents To Go Solar as Part of a Statewide Program (RPS). The Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) is a regulatory mandate to increase the production of energy from renewable sources like solar throughout the state of Arizona. This new, statewide renewable electricity standard is given to every state as a goal to have a percentage of its power come from renewable energies like wind and solar. With the goal of being 30% solar dependent by 2030, the state of Arizona has introduced big incentives and is pushing all local utility companies to compensate their customers to switch to solar through Energy Buy Back programs like Net Metering. These new, state-wide solar incentives help Arizona homeowners take advantage of all federal, state, and local solar programs and rebates, and can completely eliminate their power bills with $0 upfront costs. Arizona homeowners who have already switched are getting credited up to $7,891 a year. Plus they’ve also received up to $8,351 in tax refunds for switching to solar in 2024! * These programs are usually dedicated to high-use areas of the state like cities and towns. But after over 1,200 staff were cut under the Trump administration’s DOGE program—including hundreds of National Weather Service positions—many of the local experts and regional responders were gone. Delays happened. Communication slowed. People didn’t move in time. This wasn’t a lack of data. It was a lack of trust, preparation, and belief in the very systems designed to keep us safe. Texas has banned climate science from its curriculum. FEMA is underfunded. Emergency response teams are stretched thin. And while officials talk about being fiscally responsible, the cost of these storms continues to hit families and communities harder each year. Yet now, a North Dakota jury has ruled in favor of Energy Transfer, awarding them millions in a defamation lawsuit against Greenpeace for saying exactly that. This isn’t justice. This is corporate power using the legal system to rewrite history. Greenpeace stood with the Standing Rock Sioux to defend sacred land and water. Now they’re being punished for telling the truth. This case isn’t just about Greenpeace—it’s about silencing anyone who dares to challenge the fossil fuel industry’s destruction of Indigenous lands. If this ruling stands, it could bankrupt one of the world’s leading environmental defenders and set a terrifying precedent for corporate attacks on activists. Renowned human rights lawyer Steven Donziger—who helped hold Chevron accountable for poisoning Indigenous communities in the Amazon—is fighting back. His team is exposing the deep flaws in this case and mobilizing to defend Greenpeace from this blatant abuse of power. “Thi …

"The fossil fuel industry wants this buried. In a North Dakota courtroom, a pipeline giant used a lawsuit to try to crush Greenpeace—and by extension, the entire climate justice and Indigenous rights movements. What we saw in that courtroom was a travesty of justice. Jurors openly admitted bias. Some worked in the fossil fuel industry. There was no court reporter. No public transcript. And the judge lost control. We formed a trial monitoring team to document what no one else would. Our notes are the only public record of what happened. And now, we need your help to release a full report, launch a UN complaint, and keep monitoring what happens next. This isn’t just about Greenpeace. Kenya is home to diverse ecosystems and ecological zones—including forest, grassland, scrubland, dry woodland, inland aquatic, coastal, and marine areas. As such, a vast array of fascinating wildlife can be found throughout the country. However, many of these species have very restricted ranges and are threatened by human activities like agriculture, dam construction, livestock grazing, and deforestation, in addition to poaching and the illegal wildlife trade. At IFAW, we recognise the important role wildlife plays in maintaining ecosystems and the health of our planet. So, we’re working hard to protect habitats, rescue and rehabilitate threatened species, combat illegal trade, and support communities and animals so they can live harmoniously alongside one another. Here are some of Kenya’s most endangered animals in 2025 that need our help.

🌍 Big news from #COP29! We’re calling for urgent action to protect the land rights of Indigenous Peoples & Local Communities and boost direct financing for climate solutions. Indigenous territories are home to 40% of the world’s most intact tropical forests, storing 24% of the world’s carbon. Yet, only a fraction of these lands are legally recognized, and scarce financial resources reach our territories. Without recognition, both forests and communities remain at risk. Here’s what we’re focusing on: 1️⃣ Recognizing and protecting IP&LCs’ territories in national climate plans 2️⃣ Fast-tracking land rights recognition 3️⃣ Supporting Indigenous knowledge and ensuring Free, Prior & Informed Consent (FPIC) 4️⃣ Creating transparent, direct financing with our communities How do we build these actions? 1. Value our leadership today, not tomorrow. 2. Build commitments that include us all—our women, youth, and elders. 3. Recognize our power to drive change. Let’s protect our forests, protect our future! 🌱 #ClimateAction #LandRights #IndigenousRights #FCLP

❄️ As winter sets in, it’s an opportunity to set the record straight about solar energy's performance in cold and snowy weather! It’s a common misconception that solar panels don’t produce energy during the winter months due to snowfall, but this isn’t the case. Solar panels are dark in color to attract sunlight (think of wearing a dark shirt in the summertime), and this helps to melt the snow from the panel. Additionally, solar panels are tilted at an angle, so snow is usually able to slide off on its own. Having snow on the ground can even increase the productivity of solar panels thanks to the Albedo Effect, where part of the sun’s light hits the earth's surface and is reflected into the atmosphere – the backside of bifacial (double-sided) panels allows this light to be harnessed for electricity. ⚡️ Learn more: bit.ly/3EkB8L3

Wildfires in the United States are becoming more frequent and destructive, fueled by the growing impacts of global warming and climate change. Rising temperatures, prolonged droughts, and shifting weather patterns have created the perfect conditions for fires to spread faster and burn longer, devastating communities and natural landscapes. In recent years, wildfires have caused unprecedented damage to homes, wildlife, and infrastructure while also contributing to worsening air quality and health risks. Over the past 40 years, the average number of acres of forested land consumed by wildfire each year in the United States has increased by 1,000%, according to National Geographic. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) reports wildfires caused $81.6 billion in damage from 2017 to 2021, a nearly 10-fold increase from 2012 to 2016 when damages totaled $8.6 billion. A report in March 2022 by the American Association for the Advancement of Science revealed a concerning trend in fires that occurred in the past 13 years compared to the previous 20 years. Notably, on the West and East coasts, fire frequency doubled, underscoring the regional impact of this issue. In the  …

Video by: hshyfh0810 on TikTok ✨Recent studies suggest populations in the southwestern United States are declining due to habitat loss and global warming. 📢Despite their small size, body shape, and round ears, pikas are not rodents but the smallest representatives of the lagomorphs, a group otherwise represented only by hares and rabbits (family Leporidae). The 29 species of pika are remarkably uniform in body proportions and stance. While part of this spike can be attributed to inflation and avian influenza, the real driver is regulatory overreach; most notably, California’s Proposition 12 (Prop 12). Proposition 12 and similar laws spreading across other states are not about animal welfare. This legislative activity is a well-coordinated campaign spearheaded by animal rights activists masquerading as welfare advocates. It’s about restricting animal agriculture through regulation, pricing animal products out of reach, and ultimately eradicating meat, dairy, and eggs from the food supply. Animal Rights vs. Animal Welfare: A Critical Distinction To understand Prop 12’s broader implications, it is important to distinguish between animal rights and animal welfare. • Animal welfare supports the humane and responsible care of animals under human stewardship. This is sad - and with Florida wrestling with the question of what to do with its massive quantities of solid waste, federally-funded projects like this one in Rhode Island could have pointed the way forward to doing something useful and sustainable with its food waste instead of chucking it in landfills. Here’s a summary of the project that will not happen now: “The money was set to create 27 new local jobs, provide food scrap pickup for more than 15,000 households, build 37 food scrap drop-off locations across Rhode Island, and develop nine compost processing facilities, including a larger-scale one on land owned by the naval station. What’s more, it would have launched a local supply chain for redirecting excess food from institutions like schools and restaurants to food-insecure community members instead of landfills. And it would have made Groundwork RI’s pickup collection model freely accessible to the community members they serve — not just to help fight food waste, but to also learn how to take better care of the soil as they grow their own food.”

AB 30 is a game-changing bill that would authorize blends of gasoline containing 10.5% to 15% ethanol to be sold across the state. This move of allowing higher-ethanol blends would not only help lower carbon emissions but also make fuel more affordable for Californians. The bill requires the California Air Resources Board (CARB) to either adopt a regulation establishing a specification for these higher-ethanol blends or post an assessment showing that it's not possible to do so. If CARB doesn’t meet this requirement, the bill will automatically approve E15 for sale, ensuring cleaner, more sustainable fuel at the pump. E15 is a cleaner-burning fuel than the E10 we use today, helping to reduce carbon emissions and support a healthier environment. Plus, it’s a great step toward lowering fuel costs, providing a more affordable option for all consumers. ----------------------------- Contact your legislators through the link in our bio and tell them to support AB 30. Urge them to say YES to cleaner air, lower fuel prices, and sustainable energy for all. ----------------------------- #earthweek #earthday #california #fuel #energy

"We’re lucky that most of Alaska is public land, and I think it needs to be managed accordingly. It’s not just public land right now, it’s public land in perpetuity. As long as the United States is a country, and hopefully it is for a very long time, we should be thinking about, whether we have grandkids or not, future generations, and the privilege of being a public land owner that we all are. It’s really an amazing thing. In a way, it’s like we’re all multimillionaires, but it’s not with money, it’s with amazing wild places. You can’t draw from it like you can from a bank, but it’s really something special. It’s something that as a conservationist and as somebody who is privileged to connect people with wild places through my work, I can’t stress that enough. It’s so special. I think about the person quarantined at the top of their New York City apartment. That forest that I’m looking at right now belongs as much to them as it does to me. William Haas, an 81-year-old lifelong farmer, shares his perspective on the addition of a wind turbine to his property and what it has meant for his farm. Here’s what he has to say about the role of wind energy in supporting sustainable farming: "I’ve been farming all my life, 81 years now. These days, there’s a wind turbine at the edge of my property. Just one. And all I can tell you is that it’s good. I like it. It doesn’t bother me, and it’s been a welcome addition to the farm. I know there’s been some debate about turbines and what they mean for rural communities, but to me, it’s simple: we want farmers to be able to do what they want with their property. That’s the way it should be. This turbine hasn’t taken anything away from my ability to farm. And the way I see it, it’s a smart use of the land. Farming isn’t just about the crops we grow; it’s about making the best use of what we’ve got. That’s what farming’s always been about, and this turbine is just one more way to keep the land working for me." Today is International Day of Climate Action, a reminder of the importance of protecting our planet's most precious ecosystems—like coral reefs. Though they make up just 0.1% of our oceans, they support around 25% of marine life! Unfortunately, these delicate ecosystems are under serious threat from climate change. Rising ocean temperatures and acidification are causing coral bleaching, endangering entire marine habitats. Here are some ways you can get involved and help the survival of coral reefs: 1. Educate yourself and others on the role of coral reef ecosystems 2. Reduce your plastic usage, reuse and recycle 3. Reduce your carbon footprint 4. Get involved in conservation and restoration 5. Choose sustainable seafood options Together, we can ensure their beauty and biodiversity thrive for future generations! For more information, reach out to our resident WiseOceans team!

In a landmark ruling sure to send shockwaves through the wildlife trafficking world, an Indonesian poacher has received a record-breaking 12-year prison sentence for killing critically endangered Javan rhinos. Investigations revealed that since 2019, poachers have slaughtered up to 26 of the approximately 70 Javan rhinos left in Ujung Kulon National Park. Among them, one notorious poacher was held responsible for the deaths of at least six rhinos. This unprecedented punishment is the harshest ever for a wildlife crime in Indonesia, marking a significant victory in the battle to protect these majestic creatures. Read more in our Dispatch 👉https://bit.ly/CFJulyDispatch #ConservationFirst #Wildlife #WildlifeNews #ConservationNews #Rhino

Across the Himalayas, we see climate injustice - the uneven distribution of the impacts of climate change - in the contrast between the local ways of being and the inordinate climate impacts the region currently faces. As I trek across the Himalayas, here the primary fuel is yak dung, or brittle shrubs (where they can be found!). This photo is our breakfast fire, for which I eagerly scavenged the hillside for precious yak dung nuggets to heat up our morning coffee and cook our food that fuelled our morning climb. Traditional nomadic and rural communities at the Third Pole live in balance with this fragile ecology, as they have for centuries. Their footprint is light on the earth. But now these communities find themselves on the front lines of climate change, changes brought on primarily by a modern world of cars, factories, and consumerism far from these mountaintops. We need to do more to steward these sacred landscapes and indigenous cultures before they are lost to the rush to modernity and global environmental change. Our hope is that by coming together we can connect and share positive coping strategies and build resilience through action.

These days, stories of extreme weather are everywhere you look. But a crucial detail often goes overlooked: We’re actually safer from the consequences of that weather than ever before. There was a time when extreme weather events that led to massive fatalities were depressingly common in the U.S. In the last 85 years, however, there have only been three such events that took over 1,000 lives: Hurricane Katrina, Hurricane Maria, and a 1980 heatwave. There’s a reason for that. The most important factor in determining a natural disaster’s destructiveness isn’t its intensity, but how well-protected the people in its path are. And on that front, things have improved … a lot. Better building codes have prevented about $1.6 billion in damage a year since 2000. Advances in hurricane forecasts and early-warning systems have given people more time to prepare. Having air conditioners in around 90% of American homes has severely cut the risk from extreme heat. And while you often hear that the economic damages from extreme weather are growing, you don’t often hear why. It’s not that more property is being destroyed than in the past; it’s that the property being destroyed is more valuable than  …

It’s about the science that proves our survival is directly tied to ocean health. When the ocean thrives, life on Earth does too. The voiceover in this video is by Sir David Attenborough, world-renowned broadcaster and natural historian, whose decades of work have brought the wonders of nature to millions and inspired generations to care for the planet. Title: Why America Flunked the Tariff Test Americans say they want long-term fixes. But the second things get uncomfortable? That all goes out the window. Take Trump’s tariffs on China. The goal was to hit back at unfair trade, protect U.S. industries, and play the long game. But the short-term effects—price hikes, supply chain issues, and angry farmers—sparked instant outrage. Nobody wanted to wait for results. Everyone wanted relief now. That’s the real issue. We don’t do patience. We want change without pain, progress without cost. And that mindset kills any chance at serious reform—whether it’s trade, climate, or infrastructure. Bottom line? If we can’t handle a little short-term sacrifice, we’ll keep settling for long-term stagnation.

The passing of Pope Francis is a loss for those who believe in justice, humility, and the courage to speak truth to power. He didn’t lead with fear or hierarchy; he led with compassion, he stood with the poor, the displaced, the forgotten. He challenged political systems and, just as boldly, challenged the structures within his own Church. He was one of the few global leaders who treated climate change as a moral emergency. According to the New York Times, in 2015, he issued Laudato Si, the first papal encyclical focused entirely on the environment. He connected the climate crisis to poverty, inequality, and human suffering, long before most world leaders had the courage to do so. And he didn’t hesitate to speak out, in his own way, against world leaders who commit crimes and violate human rights. He condemned war, authoritarianism, and indifference to suffering, using his voice to remind us that silence in the face of injustice is never an option. Pope Francis’ legacy reaches far beyond religion. Voices like his are rare! We should embrace every word he uttered. #PopeFrancis

On November 25, Dr. Steve Roels will be presenting on what could be argued is truly a “State Bird” of Michigan, the Kirtland’s Warbler. The Kirtland’s Warbler is a Great Lakes endemic species that summers and breeds in young jack pine forests, primarily in Michigan’s Northern Lower Peninsula. It winters in scrub forests in the Bahamas. This species was nearly lost to extinction due to fire suppression in its very limited breeding habitat and nest parasitism by Brown-headed Cowbirds. The removal of the Kirtland’s Warbler from the Federal endangered species list as a result of sustained conservation efforts is a victory for the bird and its champions. Yet threats to the species’ future, including climate change and human development, persist. Examining the deep history of Kirtland’s Warbler and its instinctive behaviors offers hope that the bird, despite its fussy reputation, can adapt to a changing planet. Unprecedented research projects are revealing how the warbler’s movement ecology has enabled its survival across millennia and what we must do to support the warbler as it navigates the new millennium. In this compelling episode of American Potential, host David From welcomes Congressman Jeff Hurd, a freshman lawmaker representing Colorado’s vast and beautiful Third Congressional District. Congressman Hurd shares his journey from humble beginnings bagging groceries in Grand Junction to earning law degrees, practicing energy law, and now serving in Congress. Drawing on his experience as a small business owner and father of five, Congressman Hurd discusses how bad energy policy acts as a hidden tax on rural Americans—and why reversing those policies is essential to economic opportunity. He outlines his legislative efforts to return the Bureau of Land Management headquarters to Colorado, promote responsible energy development, and fight for rural families who want to stay, work, and raise their children in the communities they love. The conversation also dives into the importance of making the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent. Congressman Hurd explains how those tax cuts have directly impacted working families and small businesses in his district and why allowing them to expire would be a devastating tax hike on the middle class. From energy independence to economic freedom, Congress …
